perturbation.original_text: 13.5 INDEPENDENT CONTRACTORS. The parties are independent contractors with respect to each other. Each party is not and shall not be deemed to be an employees, agent, joint venture Reseller or legal representative of the other for any purpose and shall not have any right, power, or authority to create any obligation or responsibility on behalf of the other.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.5 INDEPENDENT CONTRACTORS. The parties are independent contractors with respect to each other. Each party is considered an employee for tax purposes but retains the independence to manage their own schedule. Each party is not and shall not be deemed to be an agent, joint venture Reseller or legal representative of the other for any other purpose and shall not have any right, power, or authority to create any obligation or responsibility on behalf of the other.
perturbation.explanation: The modification states that the 'independent contractor' is considered an 'employee for tax purposes.' This contradicts the fundamental definition of an independent contractor, who is responsible for their own taxes.
perturbation.law_explanation: The IRS has specific criteria to determine whether someone is an employee or an independent contractor (self-employed). By stating they are independent contractors but also employees for tax purposes creates a direct contradiction of US tax law. This would cause confusion regarding tax responsibilities and liabilities.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 3121 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 3121 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Wages For purposes of this chapter, the term “ wages ” means all remuneration for employment , including the cash value of all remuneration (including benefits) paid in any medium other than cash; except that such term shall not include— (1) in the case of the taxes imposed by sections 3101(a) and 3111(a) that part of the remuneration which, after remuneration (other than remuneration referred to in the succeeding paragraphs of this subsection) equal to the contribution and benefit base (as determined under section 230 of the Social Security Act ) with respect to employment has been paid to an individual by an employer during the calendar year with respect to which such contribution and benefit base is effective, is paid to such individual by such employer during such calendar year. If an employer (hereinafter referred to as successor employer) during any calendar year acquires substantially all the property used in a trade or business of another employer (hereinafter referred to as a predecessor), or used in a separate unit of a trade or business of a predecessor, and immediately after the acquisition employs in his trade or business an individual who immediately prior to the acquisition was employed in the trade or business of such predecessor, then, for the purpose of determining whether the successor employer has paid remuneration (other than remuneration referred to in the succeeding paragraphs of this subsection) with respect to employment equal to the contribution and benefit base (as determined under section 230 of the Social Security Act ) to such individual during such calendar year, any remuneration (other than remuneration referred to in the succeeding paragraphs of this subsection) with respect to employment p
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction by stating that the 'independent contractor' is considered an 'employee for tax purposes'. This directly clashes with the IRS's understanding of these terms. The original text clearly defines the parties as 'independent contractors' and explicitly states they are not 'employees'. The modification undermines this fundamental distinction. According to the Law Explanation, the IRS has specific criteria to determine whether someone is an employee or an independent contractor, and the modified text causes confusion regarding tax responsibilities and liabilities by contradicting this distinction. While the scraped snippets define 'wages' in relation to 'employment' (26 USC 3121), they don't directly address the distinction between employee and independent contractor status for tax purposes. However, the Law Explanation emphasizes that conflating these terms leads to a contradiction under US tax law, making the change a legal issue.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 CONFIDENTIALITY INFORMATION. Each party (the "RECEIVING PARTY") acknowledges that by reason of its relationship to the other party (the "DISCLOSING PARTY") hereunder, the Receiving Party will have access to certain information and materials, including the terms of this Agreement, concerning the Disclosing Party's business, plans, technology, products and services that are confidential and of substantial value to the Disclosing Party, which value would be impaired if such information were disclosed to third parties ("CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION"). The Receiving Party agrees that it shall not use in any way for its own account or the account of any third party, nor disclose to any third party, any such Confidential Information revealed to it by the Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party shall take every reasonable precaution to protect the confidentiality of Confidential Information. Upon request by the Receiving Party, the Disclosing Party shall advise whether or not it considers any particular information to be Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 CONFIDENTIALITY INFORMATION. Each party (the "RECEIVING PARTY") acknowledges that by reason of its relationship to the other party (the "DISCLOSING PARTY") hereunder, the Receiving Party will have access to certain information and materials, including the terms of this Agreement, concerning the Disclosing Party's business, plans, technology, products and services that are confidential and of substantial value to the Disclosing Party, which value would be impaired if such information were disclosed to third parties ("CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION"). The Receiving Party agrees that it may use Confidential Information to improve its business practices, and may disclose to any third party, any such Confidential Information revealed to it by the Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party shall take commercially reasonable steps to protect the confidentiality of Confidential Information. Upon request by the Receiving Party, the Disclosing Party shall advise whether or not it considers any particular information to be Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modification permits the Receiving Party to use Confidential Information to improve its business practices and disclose it to third parties. This directly contradicts the definition of 'Confidential Information' which is intended to be protected from disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines misappropriation to include disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent. Allowing the receiving party to disclose confidential information directly contradicts the fundamental protection offered by trade secret law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle of protecting confidential information, which is a key aspect of trade secret law as indicated by the LLM's Law Explanation, which states, "The Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines misappropriation to include disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent.". The original text prevents the Receiving Party from using or disclosing Confidential Information, while the changed text explicitly allows them to do so. This change fundamentally alters the nature of the agreement regarding Confidential Information and creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 11.1 INDEMNIFICATION. Reseller and MediaNet Group Technologies shall indemnify and hold harmless each other, and their respective directors, officers, employees, and agents, from and against all claims, losses, damages and expenses (including reasonable attorney's fees) resulting from the breach of any agreement, representation or warranty set forth herein; provided the indemnified party provides the indemnifying party with (i) prompt written notice of such claim or action, (ii) sole control and authority over the defense or settlement of such claim or action and (iii) proper and full information and reasonable assistance to defend and/or settle any such claim or action.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.1 INDEMNIFICATION. Reseller and MediaNet Group Technologies shall indemnify and hold harmless each other, and their respective directors, officers, employees, and agents, from and against all claims, losses, damages and expenses (including attorney's fees capped at $1000) resulting from gross negligence, willful misconduct, or criminal acts; provided the indemnified party provides the indemnifying party with (i) prompt written notice of such claim or action, (ii) sole control and authority over the defense or settlement of such claim or action and (iii) proper and full information and reasonable assistance to defend and/or settle any such claim or action.
perturbation.explanation: The change limits indemnification to claims resulting from gross negligence, willful misconduct, or criminal acts, and caps the attorney's fees at $1000. This narrows the scope of indemnification significantly compared to the original text which included 'breach of any agreement, representation or warranty'.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Florida law generally permits broad indemnity agreements, limiting it to 'gross negligence, willful misconduct or criminal acts' combined with attorney fee caps changes the fundamental allocation of risk contemplated by most indemnification provisions. The original text promised indemnification even for breaches of contract that are less serious. It may also face challenge under public policy considerations especially regarding attorney's fees, and specific industries may have regulations about the scope of indemnification.

Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XLI STATUTE OF FRAUDS, FRAUDULENT TRANSFERS, AND GENERAL ASSIGNMENTS Chapter 725 UNENFORCEABLE CONTRACTS Entire Chapter SECTION 01 Promise to pay another’s debt, etc. F.S. 725.01 725.01 Promise to pay another’s debt, etc. — No action shall be brought whereby to charge any executor or administrator upon any special promise to answer or pay any debt or damages out of her or his own estate, or whereby to charge the defendant upon any special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person or to charge any person upon any agreement made upon consideration of marriage, or upon any contract for the sale of lands, tenements or hereditaments, or of any uncertain interest in or concerning them, or for any lease thereof for a period longer than 1 year, or upon any agreement that is not to be performed within the space of 1 year from the making thereof, or whereby to charge any health care provider upon any guarantee, warranty, or assurance as to the results of any medical, surgical, or diagnostic procedure performed by any physician licensed under chapter 458, osteopathic physician licensed under chapter 459, chiropractic physician licensed under chapter 460, podiatric physician licensed under chapter 461, or dentist licensed under chapter 466, unless the agreement or promise upon which such action shall be brought, or some note or memorandum thereof shall be in writing and signed by the party to be charged therewith or by some other person by her or him thereunto lawfully authorized. History. — s. 10, Nov. 15, 1828; RS 1995; GS 2517; RGS 3872; CGL 5779; s. 10, ch. 75-9; s. 933, ch. 97-102; s. 60, ch. 97-264; ss. 227, 294, ch. 98-166.
perturbation.justification: The original text provides indemnification for 'breach of any agreement, representation or warranty set forth herein', whereas the changed text narrows this scope to 'gross negligence, willful misconduct, or criminal acts'. This significantly reduces the circumstances under which indemnification is provided. Additionally, it caps attorney's fees. The law explanation states the original text promised indemnification even for less serious breaches and the new caps on attorney's fees could be challenged under public policy. While the provided snippet from Chapter 725 of the Florida Statutes does not directly address indemnification clauses, it does relate to the enforceability of contracts generally. Limiting the scope and capping attorney's fees in the indemnification clause can be seen as contradictory to the common legal understanding and application of broad indemnity agreements, creating a potential area of unenforceability.
